description: Fix missing activity indicator (green blinking dot) for browser tools in the side panel. Plugin tools (e.g., discord_send_message) correctly show a blinking green dot on the plugin icon and tool icon during invocation, but browser tools (e.g., browser_list_tabs) show nothing. The root cause is that handleBrowserToolCall in mcp-tool-dispatch.ts does not call sendInvocationStart/sendInvocationEnd, so the side panel never receives tool.invocationStart/tool.invocationEnd notifications for browser tools. The side panel UI is already wired up to display activity for browser tools (BrowserToolsCard.tsx expects keys like 'browser:browser_list_tabs' in activeTools), but these keys are never populated.

userStories.title: Add sendInvocationStart/End to handleBrowserToolCall
userStories.description: As a user, I want to see the green blinking activity indicator on the browser Chrome icon and browser tool icons in the side panel when browser tools are invoked, so that I have visual feedback that a tool is executing.
acceptanceCriteria: handleBrowserToolCall calls sendInvocationStart(state, 'browser', toolName) before executing the tool handler
handleBrowserToolCall calls sendInvocationEnd(state, 'browser', toolName, durationMs, success) in the finally block after execution, matching the same pattern used in handlePluginToolCall
sendInvocationEnd is called with success=false when the tool handler throws an error
sendInvocationEnd is called with success=false when the tool is disabled, permission denied, or validation fails (early returns before execution)
Existing unit tests for handleBrowserToolCall still pass
New unit tests verify sendInvocationStart is called once and sendInvocationEnd is called once with correct args for both success and error cases
userStories.notes:
The fix is in platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-tool-dispatch.ts, function handleBrowserToolCall (lines 167-302). Follow the exact same pattern as handlePluginToolCall (lines 310-492) which calls sendInvocationStart at line 393 and sendInvocationEnd at line 482 in the finally block.

The key insight: sendInvocationStart/sendInvocationEnd are imported from extension-protocol.ts (lines 12-13 of mcp-tool-dispatch.ts). They take (state, plugin, tool) and (state, plugin, tool, durationMs, success) respectively. For browser tools, use plugin='browser' and tool=toolName (the full browser tool name like 'browser_list_tabs').

The side panel already expects keys of the form 'browser:${toolName}' — see BrowserToolsCard.tsx line 113 and line 168. The VALID_PLUGIN_NAME regex (/^[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$/) in platform/browser-extension/src/constants.ts line 94 matches 'browser'.

IMPORTANT: There are multiple early return paths in handleBrowserToolCall (disabled tool at line 174, validation failure at line 183, permission denied at line 200, confirmation denied at line 242, confirmation timeout at line 257, confirmation error at line 268). For simplicity, place sendInvocationStart BEFORE the tool handler execution (line 275) and sendInvocationEnd in the existing finally block (lines 292-301). The early returns for validation/permission don't need invocation notifications — they never reach the execution phase. This matches conceptually with how plugin tools work: sendInvocationStart is called after validation succeeds.

Actually, looking more carefully at handlePluginToolCall, sendInvocationStart is called at line 393 AFTER concurrency checks but BEFORE the try block that does actual dispatch. The analogous place in handleBrowserToolCall is just before line 275 (const btStartTs = Date.now()). sendInvocationEnd should go in the existing finally block alongside appendAuditEntry.

For unit tests, look at the existing test file platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-tool-dispatch.test.ts. The test at line 908 ('sendInvocationStart and sendInvocationEnd are called') tests plugin tools. Add analogous tests for browser tools. The mock setup at line 230-231 already mocks sendInvocationStart and sendInvocationEnd. Find the browser tool test section and add tests verifying these are called with plugin='browser'.

userStories.title: Add E2E test for browser tool activity indicator in side panel
userStories.description: As a developer, I want E2E test coverage verifying that browser tool invocations show the green blinking activity indicator in the side panel, so that regressions are caught automatically.
acceptanceCriteria: New E2E test in e2e/side-panel-data-flow.e2e.ts verifies that invoking a browser tool (e.g., browser_list_tabs) causes the animate-activity-flash CSS class to appear on the browser tools section
Test verifies the activity dot disappears after the tool call completes
Test follows the same pattern as the existing plugin tool activity indicator test at line 358 of side-panel-data-flow.e2e.ts
userStories.notes:
The existing E2E test for plugin tool activity is in e2e/side-panel-data-flow.e2e.ts at line 358: 'shows activity indicator during tool call and removes it after'. This test uses the e2e-test plugin's echo tool with a slow mode (3s delay) to make the activity indicator observable.

For browser tools, the challenge is that most browser tools execute very quickly (milliseconds), making the activity indicator hard to catch. Two approaches:

1. (Preferred) Use a browser tool that takes some time — e.g., browser_screenshot_tab or plugin_analyze_site. But even these may be too fast.

2. (Alternative) Use the side panel to observe that the activity indicator appeared at all. The side panel tracks activeTools state. You could check the BrowserToolsCard for the animate-activity-flash class appearing during the tool call.

The test structure should:
1. Open the side panel (using openSidePanel helper from e2e/helpers.ts)
2. Expand the Browser Tools accordion in the side panel
3. Start a browser tool call (e.g., browser_list_tabs via MCP client.callTool)
4. Assert the animate-activity-flash class appears on the browser tool row or the browser Chrome icon
5. Wait for the tool call to complete
6. Assert the activity indicator disappears

Since browser tools are fast, you may need to use Promise.all to concurrently call the tool and watch for the indicator. The existing test at line 437-440 does exactly this pattern with Promise.all.

Look at how the existing test locates the activity indicator: it finds the tool row and then looks for the animate-activity-flash class within it. For browser tools, the BrowserToolsCard renders tool rows with ToolIcon components that have the same activity indicator behavior (see BrowserToolsCard.tsx line 168).

Key files:
- e2e/side-panel-data-flow.e2e.ts (add new test in the 'tool invocation animation' describe block or a new describe block)
- e2e/helpers.ts (existing helpers like openSidePanel, setupToolTest, etc.)
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/BrowserToolsCard.tsx (to understand the DOM structure for locator queries)

IMPORTANT: Browser tools don't go through the extension adapter — they execute server-side or via chrome.* APIs. The invocation notifications are sent over the WebSocket from the MCP server to the extension. The side panel receives them via the message-router forwarding. So the E2E test setup is simpler than plugin tools — no need for the e2e-test plugin's slow mode. But you DO need the side panel open and connected to see the indicators.

If the activity indicator is too fast to catch with a locator assertion, consider: (a) checking that the activity flash CSS class was applied at any point during the call using a MutationObserver approach, or (b) testing with a slower browser tool. Another option: the browser Chrome icon (PluginIcon with pluginName='browser') also gets an activity indicator (hasActiveTool prop) — this might be easier to locate in the DOM.
